title: Per capita plastic waste vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Per capita plastic waste generation rate is measured in kilograms per person per day. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Per capita plastic waste vs. GDP per capita

Per capita plastic waste generation rate is measured in kilograms per person per day. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot displays the relationship between per capita plastic waste generation and GDP per capita for the year 2010. The vertical axis measures plastic waste in kilograms per person per day, ranging from 0 kg to over 0.6 kg. The horizontal axis represents GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices, plotted on a logarithmic scale from $1,000 to $100,000. Data points are color-coded by continent, with North America in orange, South America in maroon, Africa in purple, Europe in blue, Asia in teal, and Oceania in light blue. Circle sizes correspond to population, with India and China appearing as the largest circles near the bottom of the chart.

Countries with the highest plastic waste generation, exceeding 0.5 kg per person per day, include Antigua and Barbuda, Kuwait, and Guyana. These nations span different GDP levels, with Kuwait situated near the $50,000 mark while Guyana is closer to $10,000. A cluster of high-income nations, including the United States, Germany, and the Netherlands, sits in the upper-right quadrant with GDPs above $40,000 and waste generation between 0.3 kg and 0.5 kg. In contrast, many African nations such as the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Togo, and Tanzania cluster in the bottom-left corner, showing both low GDP (under $2,000) and low waste generation (under 0.1 kg). Large Asian populations like India and Indonesia also appear in the lower range of waste generation despite having GDPs between $5,000 and $10,000. The distribution shows a general trend where waste generation increases with GDP, though significant variation exists within income groups.
